text: The cue in a habit loop can be drawn from five categories: location, time, emotional state, other people, or an immediately preceding action. Duhigg argues that cues are often invisible to us precisely because we are inside the routines they trigger — we rarely notice a behavior's starting gun. Identifying the cue is therefore the first and hardest step of habit change. Behaviorist B.F. Skinner had earlier shown that stimulus control — the power of environmental cues to elicit conditioned responses — was stronger and more reliable than willpower. The implication is stark: redesigning your environment is more effective than resolving to behave differently.
question: According to the passage, why are cues often invisible to the person experiencing them?

options.text: Cues are stored in long-term memory and cannot be consciously accessed
People are already inside the routines the cues trigger by the time they notice them
The basal ganglia suppresses cue recognition to preserve cognitive energy
Skinner showed that cues only become visible after the reward is received
